title: Pricing & Revenue Model
READ_THIS_FIRST: This file gives modeled price BANDS and revenue-model LOGIC for each of the three phases, not confirmed prices. None of the numbers below are 'verified_external' — every one is tagged 'modeled_projection' or 'assumption' and is a planning anchor, not a committed price list. The Phase 3 hardware layer keeps the prior discipline exactly: no price appears there until Phase 0 discovery supplies a real cost-to-serve and a measured willingness to pay. The Phase 1/2 software layer is new, and its bands are modeled against comparable freemium/agri-SaaS pricing patterns, not against this company's own data, because none exists yet. A founder who says 'here is our pricing logic and the bands we are testing toward' is more credible than one who presents a confident price list built on zero users.

the_three_phase_pricing_structure.phase_1: Free / near-free — the acquisition tier. No monetisation.
the_three_phase_pricing_structure.phase_2: Freemium premium subscription + marketplace take-rate + consultation fees — the monetisation tier.
the_three_phase_pricing_structure.phase_3: Hardware-as-a-service deployment pricing — the enterprise tier, priced against loss avoidance and aeration savings rather than hardware cost, exactly as in the original hardware-first plan.

what_it_includes: AI-assisted fish farming guide and setup assistant
phase_1_foundation_pricing.why_free_not_near_free: Any price, however small, reintroduces the affordability barrier this tier exists to remove, and adds payment-friction cost (processing, support tickets, churn at the paywall) that a true free tier avoids. 'Near-free' is kept in the model name only as a fallback if Phase 0-equivalent app discovery finds a reason a token price improves retention (e.g. loss-aversion effects reported in some freemium literature) — but the default plan is USD/PHP 0.
phase_1_foundation_pricing.cost_to_serve_this_tier: Hosting and inference cost per free user, modeled at a few US cents to roughly USD 0.50 per active user per month depending on AI feature usage (disease-ID inference is the most compute-intensive free feature). NOT MEASURED — no real usage data exists yet.

premium_subscription.how_this_band_was_modeled: Anchored against two reference points, neither of which is company data: (1) comparable freemium farm-management / agri-SaaS apps globally typically price premium tiers in the low-tens-of-dollars-per-month range; (2) the pricing philosophy carried over from the original plan — price near value the farmer can VERIFY (time saved vs. manual logging, feed-cost savings from better FCR) rather than against the harder-to-prove predictive-health upside. The band is wide because neither anchor is measured for this specific product or market.
what_it_includes: Premium analytics and predictive health monitoring

the_pricing_inputs_still_required.input: Measured willingness to pay among Phase 1 free users who hit the upgrade prompt
Actual conversion-triggering feature (which premium feature people upgrade FOR)
Local purchasing power in the primary launch geography (Philippines) vs. a USD-denominated price
the_pricing_inputs_still_required.note: A USD 5-25/month band may be correctly priced for a 'growing commercial farm' and badly overpriced for an aspirational smallholder who upgraded too early. Tiering or PPP-adjusted local pricing may be required.

marketplace_take_rate.how_this_band_was_modeled: Anchored against typical agri/e-commerce marketplace commission rates (commonly 5-20 percent depending on category and logistics involvement). No AquaVision-specific transaction data exists.
marketplace_take_rate.applies_to: Feed, equipment, and buyer-side transactions conducted through the in-app marketplace.
marketplace_take_rate.open_question: Whether AquaVision handles payment/logistics (justifies the higher end) or is purely a lead-referral layer (justifies the lower end). NOT DECIDED.

consultation_fees.candidate_model: Per-session fee paid by the farmer, split between AquaVision and the consulting aquaculturist/vet.
consultation_fees.how_this_band_was_modeled: Anchored against typical telehealth/expert-marketplace session pricing and platform take-rates in adjacent gig-expert marketplaces. No aquaculture-specific comparable was found and none of this is measured against actual willingness to pay for aquaculture consultation specifically.
consultation_fees.the_supply_side_risk: This stream requires recruiting and retaining paid aquaculturists/vets willing to work through the platform — an unvalidated two-sided marketplace problem, not just a pricing problem.

phase_3_smart_aquaculture_pricing.carried_forward_from_the_original_hardwa: The logic below is unchanged from the prior single-phase version of this file — it was already disciplined and remains correct for the Phase 3 hardware-as-a-service layer.

pricing_philosophy.principle: Price against value delivered, not cost incurred — anchor on the value the customer can VERIFY, not the value we claim.
pricing_philosophy.why: Verifiable value (aeration savings, audit-labour savings) is a floor the customer will accept because they can check it. Claimed value (avoided mortality) is a ceiling we cannot prove. Price near the verifiable floor to win the deal, and let the unverifiable upside be the reason they renew once they have seen it.

reasoning: A five-figure-USD capital ask to a fish farm is a non-starter on affordability and gadget-fatigue.
Selling the hardware transfers maintenance and calibration responsibility to a customer who cannot do it, which destroys the data quality the whole product depends on.
Service retention keeps the data relationship, which is the moat.
should_we_sell_the_hardware_outright.model: AquaVision owns the hardware; the customer buys the monitoring service — hardware-as-a-service, not equipment sales.

the_pricing_inputs_required.input: Cost to serve per site per period
the_pricing_inputs_required.source: Real BOM + measured maintenance labour + dock amortisation + battery opex + field ops

the_pricing_inputs_required.note: The verifiable-value anchor; possibly the single most important pricing input.

the_pricing_inputs_required.input: Feed spend and current FCR per operator

the_pricing_inputs_required.note: Drives per-cage pricing and the buoy-crossover threshold.

candidate_pricing_structures.verdict: Essential for the smallholder-adjacent hardware segment (co-op amortises one dock across members).
Covers dock siting and setup cost distinct from ongoing subscription.
A distinct and important stream, not a substitute for commercial revenue.
Aspirational. Interesting once validated; dangerous before.

revenue_projection_status.statement: No hardware revenue projection appears here.
revenue_projection_status.why: It would require a price (unknown), a customer count (unknown), and a conversion rate (unknown), multiplied into a number that would look authoritative and be meaningless. See financial-projections.json.

cross_phase_notes.why_no_single_price_list: Three genuinely different pricing problems sit under one brand: a zero-price acquisition tier, a freemium conversion problem with an unmeasured willingness-to-pay, and a hardware-as-a-service problem that requires a real BOM before any number is honest. Collapsing them into one pricing page would hide which numbers are guessable now (Phase 1/2, against comparable software businesses) and which are genuinely unknowable before physical-world discovery (Phase 3).
cross_phase_notes.when_this_gets_filled_in: Phase 1/2 bands tighten as soon as real conversion and usage data exist post-launch — weeks, not seasons. Phase 3 numbers fill in only after farmer discovery and a real sonde/drone BOM, exactly as before — this has not gotten easier just because the company also has a software product now.
